coredata.dc:title: Non-colorimetric sensing with 3,3´,5,5´-tetramethylbenzidine

coredata.dc:description: 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is by far the most well-known colorimetric substrate in various colorimetric assays, including ELISA and typical H2O2/reactive oxygen species oxidation-involved colorimetric detections. For such a substrate, besides the well-known colorimetric applications, the intriguing spectroscopic and electrochemical properties are also recognized, such as fluorescence, photothermal and photoacoustic responses, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical property. The explorations of these properties greatly expanded the sensing toolbox and fruited the applications of TMB in chemo-/bio-sensing. Therefore, in this review, we provide a summary on the TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing applications, with a particular focus on the spectroscopic and electrochemical characteristics and their corresponding principles for sensor designs. Additionally, the typical sensors were summarized and their performances were also compared. Finally, the advantages, challenges, and future perspectives for TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing applications are also presented. Considering the importance of TMB in analytical chemistry, the non-colorimetric sensing applications would be an important supplement to the well-known colorimetric assays, and may also be a good example in how to better exploring the already existed and newly developed molecules.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is by far the most well-known colorimetric substrate in various colorimetric assays, including ELISA and typical H2O2/reactive oxygen species oxidation-involved colorimetric detections. For such a substrate, besides the well-known colorimetric applications, the intriguing spectroscopic and electrochemical properties are also recognized, such as fluorescence, photothermal and photoacoustic responses, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical property. The explorations of these properties greatly expanded the sensing toolbox and fruited the applications of TMB in chemo-/bio-sensing. Therefore, in this review, we provide a summary on the TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing applications, with a particular focus on the spectroscopic and electrochemical characteristics and their corresponding principles for sensor designs. Additionally, the typical sensors were summarized and their performances were also compared. Finally, the advantages, challenges, and future perspectives for TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing applications are also presented. Considering the importance of TMB in analytical chemistry, the non-colorimetric sensing applications would be an important supplement to the well-known colorimetric assays, and may also be a good example in how to better exploring the already existed and newly developed molecules.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The non-chromogenic applications of TMB were summarized.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Benzidine is a classical substrate for various peroxidase-based colorimetric assays, but its extreme toxicity necessitates the search for safe and high-performance alternatives ( Fig. 1A). Since its first synthesis in 1974, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) gradually replaced benzidine and became the preferred substrate for peroxidases, particularly in enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA). Compared with benzidine, TMB exhibits improved catalytic efficiency and higher molar extinction coefficient (ε) ( Table 1) [1], as well as low mutagenicity and carcinogenicity [2–5]. Although 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) exhibits a higher molar extinction coefficient (ε420 nm = 43,200 M–1·cm–1), the sensitivity and robustness of TMB is still higher, probably because of its higher efficiency in catalytic oxidation and the long-term stability of its oxidation products [6,7]. Later, the outstanding sensitivity of TMB is also recognized by the communities of nanozyme [8–11], G-quadruplex DNAzyme [12–14], and photosensitization-based visual detection [15–17], thus making it the most well-known colorimetric substrate in colorimetric sensing. Using "3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine" as the keyword, the searched results from Google Scholar exhibit a steady rise over the year, which further highlights the significance of TMB (Fig. 1B).

As a signal output chromophore, TMB is also challenged with limited application scenarios and insufficient sensitivity. During the exploration of TMB as the colorimetric substrate, its diverse spectroscopic and electrochemical properties are also gradually recognized (Fig. 1C). To extend the application scenarios, the high molar extinction coefficient of its charge-transfer complex (CTC, the typical one-electron oxidation product) in the near-infrared region was explored in photothermal and photoacoustic sensing in vivo [18,19]. Meanwhile, the electrochemical properties of TMB+• and TMB2+ were also employed to engineer portable biosensors for on-site detection [20,21]. For sensitivity improvement, Resonance Raman scattering (RRS) signal from the single electron radical cation (TMB+•) and di-electron oxidation product (TMB2+) were employed in the fabrication of highly sensitive Raman biosensors [22–24]. Moreover, the intrinsic fluorescence of TMB was explored to develop fluorescent sensors for real-time monitoring targets [25,26]. The emergence of these technologies has significantly fruited the applications of TMB in biosensing, but regretfully, not as famous as its colorimetric applications.

Thus in this review, we concentrated on the non-colorimetric properties of TMB, including fluorescence, inner filter effect (IFE), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), photothermal effect (PTE), photoacoustic effect (PAE), and electrochemistry ( Fig. 2). We tried to provide a comprehensive overview of TMB in non-colorimetric sensing applications, from the fundamental mechanisms of each property to practical sensing. Specifically, we began with a presentation of the properties of TMB and its oxidation products, as well as their relationship with non-colorimetric sensing applications. In the following section, we primarily focused on the important achievements in various non-colorimetric applications. For each specific part, we first discussed the general principles of non-colorimetric applications and then introduced a diverse of examples with detailed discussions. Finally, we summarized the advantages and challenges of TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing and provided our opinions on future opportunities. We believe the current work is a timely summary supplementary to the well-known colorimetric sensing applications of TMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The general properties of TMB and its oxidation products
ce:section.ce:para: In 1974, TMB was first introduced as a colorimetric substrate in replacing the carcinogenic benzidine for horseradish peroxidase (HRP) in ELISA [2]. Typically, TMB works in an acidic media (pH ∼ 4.0), and yields two distinct colored products after oxidation [16,27]. Initially, a blue CTC, composed of TMB and its diimine oxidation product, exists in equilibrium with the radical cation of TMB+• ( Fig. 3A) [28,29]. Further oxidation or a disproportionation reaction, which facilitated by the introduction of a strong, non-oxidizing acid (e.g., diluted H2SO4), leads to the formation of a yellow diimine, known as TMB2+. Because the form of TMB2+ shows high stability under acidic conditions, it was widely taken as the eventual signal molecule in ELISA [5,30].

The non-colorimetric sensing applications are closely linked to the spectroscopic and electrochemical properties of TMB and its oxidation products (Fig. 3B and C). TMB itself exhibits weak fluorescence peaked at 427 nm. However, the fluorescence of TMB disappears after oxidation, thus enabling the construction of fluorescence-based sensors. The coexisting oxidization product of TMB, namely TMB+• and CTC, exhibits three absorption bands centered at 370 nm, 651 nm, and 902 nm, respectively. The bands at 651 nm and 902 nm are the characteristic of the CTC, whereas the assignment of the band at 370 nm remains controversial [26,31,32]. Due to its high molar extinction coefficient (651 and 902 nm) and the good photothermal conversion efficiency in the near-infrared region, the CTC has been widely used in designing sensors based on IFE, PTE, and PAE. It should be noted that the molecular transformations from TMB to TMB2+, as well as to TMB+•and CTC, would induce novel RRS signals, which are utilized in the development of SERS-based sensors [24]. Furthermore, the redox characteristics of TMB+• and TMB2+ are exploited for signal generation in electrochemical detection [33]. To conclude, the diverse and superior properties of TMB and its oxidation products are the foundation of non-colorimetric sensing applications.

Under ultraviolet light excitation, TMB emits weak fluorescence, which can be quenched via collisional deactivation or oxidation ( Fig. 4A). Yang et al. designed a liquid drop sensor for 2,4-dinitrophenol, achieving a detection limit (LOD) of 0.1 μM (Fig. 4B) [25]. With a similar principle, Yang et al. successfully detected micro-amounts of DNA [34]. Since Cr(VI) could oxidize TMB to TMB•+, a method for Cr(VI) detection was developed with a wide linear calibration range from 20 nM to 10 mM (Fig. 4C) [26]. In another work, Yu et al. developed a competitive immunoassay to detect Schistosoma japonicum antibodies, in which the oxidation of TMB into azo-dimers at neutral pH conditions resulted in a fluorescence decrease (Fig. 4D) [35].

To expand the use of TMB in fluorescence sensors, Chan et al. developed a TMB derivative named 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethyl-N-(9-anthrylmethyl) benzidine (TMAB), which exhibited enhanced fluorescence upon forming a Schiff base with n-butyraldehyde (Fig. 4E). Successfully detection of n-butyraldehyde with a dynamic detection range from 0.017 to 4.2 mM and a LOD as low as 0.003 mM was reported [36]. Similarly, utilizing the principle of the Schiff base-involved reaction, two other derivatives, TMBS and TMBB, have been used to detect CO2 and β-D-glucose through the pH-triggered release of TMB (Fig. 4F) [37].

Despite the above examples, the use of TMB fluorescence for sensing was limited, and almost all applications were against samples with simple matrices. Probably, compared to common fluorophores, the intrinsic fluorescence of TMB was weak thus resulting in low sensitivity [25,36]. Besides, the fluorescence of TMB (peaking at 427 nm) locates in the blue region, which overlaps with the background fluorescence of sample matrices. Therefore, the utilization of TMB to construct fluorescent sensors still presents significant challenges.

IFE manifests as a decrease in fluorescence intensity when another substance in the solution absorbs either the excitation or the emission light. The CTC of TMB, known for its high molar extinction coefficient and broad absorption [16,38], has been extensively used in the development of IFE-based sensors ( Fig. 5A). Theoretically, there are two approaches to construct the TMB-based IFE sensors. The first is a "turn-on" approach, which involves diminishing the amount of CTC, consequently leading to the enhanced fluorescence. The second is a "turn-off" method, where CTC is produced by TMB oxidization, and its presence is used to detect the analytes by fluorescence quenching. Hence, substances capable of either modulating the oxidation of TMB or the decrease of CTC can be detected by the TMB-based IFE sensors.

Chu et al. reported a TMB-based IFE sensor for H2O2 detection, which utilized the peroxidase-like activity of the GSH-stabilized gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) to oxidize TMB into CTC. The generated CTC reduced the fluorescence of AuNCs, thus enabling the detection of H2O2 through a “turn-off” mode [41]. Employing a similar strategy, Lu et al. developed a sensor for alkaline phosphatase (ALP), in which the ALP activity was initially harvested to generate ascorbic acid for subsequent reducing of CTC back to TMB, thus recovering the fluorescence of AuNCs (Fig. 5B) [39]. The LOD of the IFE sensor for ALP was 0.16 U/mL and was more sensitive than that of the colorimetric assay (LOD, 0.26 U/mL). Combining with novel fluorescent probes and catalytic enzymes, these strategies were successfully extended to detect multiple metal ions [42,43], small molecules [44–46], and biomacromolecules [39,47].

The diazo reaction between TMB and nitrite produces a yellow product, which was reported four decades ago [48]. Recently, it was found that the coexisting TMB+• could also react with nitrite to produce a diazotized TMB+•, shifting the color from blue to green and then yellow (Fig. 5C). On the basis of the above reaction, Wang et al. developed an IFE sensor for nitrite detection, in which the generated diazotized TMB+• quenched the fluorescence of Mn-doped N-rich carbon dots (CDs), achieving a LOD of 0.44 μM [40]. By integrating manganese-doped silicon quantum dots and a smartphone for fluorescence measurement, the simplified approach yielded an LOD of 18.3 μM for nitrite detection [49].

In essence, IFE can convert the colorimetric response of CTC into more sensitive fluorescence signal, thus is advantageous in decreasing the methodology detection limit [50]. Compared to the traditional labeled quenchers, CTC possesses a broadband absorbing region (Fig. 3B) and a strong molar extinction coefficient (ε655 nm = 39,000 M−1 cm−1). Therefore, developing fluorescent materials with strong emission, together with catalysts that exhibit high TMB oxidation activity, would be a promising approach to further enhance the performance of the IFE-based sensors.

Photothermal effect (PTE) is known as temperature rise from the absorption of photons. Specifically, a molecule absorbs a photon and is pumped to the excited state. Subsequent energy relaxation proceeds via the non-radiative process rather than light emission, leading to the generation of heat ( Fig. 6A). CTC of TMB is favored as a photothermal agent because of its high molar extinction coefficient and photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE), particularly in the near-infrared region. The photothermal property of CTC was first explored to the development of a photothermal immunoassay for prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in 2018 (Fig. 6B) [18]. Such an assay achieved a LOD equal to the traditional colorimetric methods. Combining with enzymes or nanozymes, the photothermal effect of the CTC was frequently explored for the designing of various immunosensors [19,51–54]. To facilitate the PTE-based sensors for practical applications, portable detectors, including smartphones, thermometers, lateral flow chromatography strips, and thermoelectric modules, were employed for signal readout, which permitted point-of-care testing [51,55–58].

To improve the sensitivity of the PTE-based sensors, a series of signal amplification strategies were implemented to enhance the PTE responses. For instance, Ding et al. utilized rolling circle amplification to generate an abundance of G-quadruplex-DNA-hemin conjugates, resulting in a 1000-fold decrease of LOD when compared with the traditional fluorescent ELISA (Fig. 6C) [59]. A flower-like DNA structure was constructed to elevate the loading capacity of graphene quantum dot nanozymes, leading to an eventual LOD of 2170 particles/μL for exosome detection [62]. With hybridization chain reaction, He et al. developed a sensor capable of detecting DNA with an LOD as low as 0.4 fM. The basic strategy behind these amplifications is to increase the amount of catalysts that respond to each target molecule, thereby enhancing the sensitivity.

In a closed system, the temperature rise will result in an increased pressure, thus enabling pressure-based detection. With a flexible piezoresistive pressure sensor, Huang et al. utilized the PTE of CTC to enhance the pressure changes, achieving an improved sensitivity for carcinoembryonic antigen detection (Fig. 6D) [60,63]. Besides, the elevated temperature could also facilitate molecular movement. Based on such principle, Liu et al. converted the PTE signal of CTC into a fluorescence-dependent distance signal, in which the fluorescence of perovskite was quenched by the expulsion of ammonia gas that showed a positive correlation with the solution temperature (Fig. 6E) [61]. In their another work, the authors used the increased air pressure to trigger the movement of a colored liquid within a microfluidic chip, thus permitting a visual and quantifiable assessment of glucose and cholesterol with distance signals [64].

Because the thermal signals can be read out by portable devices, e.g., thermometers and photothermal imaging device, TMB-based photothermal sensors are an ideal choice for point-of-care testing [65]. For photothermal sensing, TMB is more popular than other reagents, such as ABTS and polydopamine [66,67], since TMB is the typical substrate in the ELISA kits. Moreover, the photothermal signal could be used to detect targets in vivo, thus significantly extending the range of targets.

Photoacoustic effect (PAE) is the generation of acoustic waves from a sample upon the absorption of pulsed light. Such a process involves rapid thermal expansions and contractions induced by temperature pulsation ( Fig. 7A). Due to less scattering and deep-penetration of the sound wave (versus light), it is possible to generate clear and high-resolution images of internal biological structures, including those usually obscured by the surrounding tissues [68,69]. Additionally, such a principle has also been employed in the development of various sensors [70,71].

In 2019, the PAE of the oxidized TMB was first explored for photoacoustic imaging [72]. At a low pH level (pH 5.0), the oxidation of TMB is prone to self-assemble into charge-transfer nanocomplexes (Fig. 7B). In contrast, in alkaline conditions, such as at pH 7.4, CTCs would transform to TMB++. Such different responses led to pH-sensitive photoacoustic tumor imaging in the NIR window. Moreover, the coating of TMB onto nanomaterials (e.g., nanosheets and nanospheres) was proved to be effective for sensitivity increasing in photoacoustic detection, which enabled the imaging of the deep-seated tumors with minimal background [74–79].

Due to the simple optics in photoacoustic-based detection, portable sensing devices can thus be expected. For example, Zhang et al. designed a portable, photoacoustic-based device for the detection of uric acid (Fig. 7C) [73]. To simplify the instrumental construction to facilitate point-of-care application, a smartphone was taken as the signal detector and processor. Such an attempt significantly reduced the size and complexity of the photoacoustic device, thus paving the way for on-site detection (particularly resource-limited settings). Considering the sensing principle (uric acid → H2O2 → oxTMB), the above sensing device could be made universal for other analytes via exploring the analyte-specific oxidases.

The PAE of CTC originates from its PTE and shares nearly all the advantages. However, the application of the PAE of CTC in sensors is still in its early stages, and most PAE applications are focused on bio-imaging. Due to the outstanding features of CTCs, including NIR response, pH sensitivity, high resolution, biodegradable feature, and deep penetration (up to 5 cm tissue) [76–78], PAE-based applications are promising and future developments are thus expected.

The Resonance Raman scattering (RRS) spectroscopy for TMB+• and TMB2+ was thoroughly investigated by Misono et al. in 1997 [24]. Upon oxidation of TMB to TMB+• or TMB2+, three principal RRS bands were observed at ∼1610, 1520, and 335 cm−1, which could be attributed to a combination of ring stretching and C-H bending, inter-ring stretching, and -CH3 co-bending modes, respectively[24,80,81]. Interestingly, no appreciable RRS bands could be collected from TMB, the distinct RRS bands of TMB+• or TMB2+ thus can be harvested for RRS detection ( Fig. 8A). It should be noted that the SERS substrate is another decisive factor for SERS-based detection, controlling TMB oxidation and choosing of a proper SERS substrate are thus two fundamental steps for designing TMB-based SERS sensors.

The first TMB-based SERS sensor was reported in 2013, in which silver nanoparticles were explored to catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, followed by the adsorption of oxTMB onto the surface of AgNPs for Raman enhancing [81]. Such a method allowed the direct detection of H2O2 with a LOD of 100 nM. Similarly, Zhan et al. utilized AuNPs to amplify the Raman signal of oxTMB for the detection of respiratory syncytial virus (RSV, in ELISA format, Fig. 8B) [82]. The minimum detectable concentration of the TMB-based SERS sensor was twenty-fold lower than that of the conventional colorimetric approach, and similar superior sensitivities (SERS over colorimetric) were also observed elsewhere [22,85–88]. Thus, it can be concluded that the TMB-based SERS sensors offer greater sensitivity over the colorimetric counterparts.

To extend the versatility of TMB-based SERS sensors, nanozyme-catalyzed TMB oxidation was also employed [22,89–91]. For instance, Li et al. engineered a bifunctional Au@Pt core-shell nanozyme that exhibited superior catalytic efficacy and strong SERS activity, thus enabling sensitive detection of Salmonella typhimurium with a LOD of 10 CFU/mL (Fig. 8C) [83]. A nanocomposite that exhibited both peroxidase and SERS activities was constructed by coating silver nanoparticles onto the copper-polyphenol colloidal spheres. Since oxTMB could be reduced by glutathione, a "turn off" SERS sensor was thus developed with a LOD of 10−7 M [91]. In another study, the quenching effect of Hg2+ on the SERS activity of gold nanostars (Hg2+ induced-amalgamation) was explored for sensitive detection of Hg2+, yielding an LOD as low as 0.2 ppb for Hg2+ (Fig. 8D) [84]. Besides nanozymes, G-quadruplex DNAzyme [92] and oxidative metal ions [87,93,94], are also explored in TMB-based SERS sensors.

SERS sensors employing TMB as a sensing agent have been widely reported to detect ions, drugs, proteins, and pathogens [23,83,94–97]. Benefiting from the high sensitivity of SERS, these sensors typically exhibit lower detection limits in comparison to the traditional colorimetric assays. It should be noted that modification of SERS substrates also enables the construction of novel TMB-based sensors, largely enriching the approaches available for sensor development [83]. Moreover, the development of miniaturized Raman spectrometers makes signal readout faster and more cost-effective, significantly facilitating the application of these sensors from laboratories to practical scenarios.

Typically, electrochemical detection possesses higher sensitivity than the widely used spectrophotometric methods. The reversible redox characters of TMB, with two oxidation peaks at approximately 0.33 V (TMB+•) and 0.49 V (TMB2+) in the cyclic voltammogram, enable the conversion of the spectrophotometric signal into an electrochemical signal ( Fig. 9A) [33,98]. Previous studies indicated that the reduction current of oxidized TMB could be accurately quantified with a nearly-zero background by selecting an appropriate working potential (e.g., ∼ 0.1 V, versus Ag/AgCl), benefiting to construct ultrasensitive sensors [99,100]. Besides, as the substrate for the amperometric detection of HRP activity, TMB (36 nA μmol/L) showed a better sensitivity over the widely used hydroquinone (16 nA μmol/L) [100]. Most importantly, the independence of the cyclic voltammetry of TMB-based amperometric detection could significantly simplify the testing apparatus, thus making the tests available away from a centralized laboratory [101–105].

Employing an ELISA format, the first TMB-based electrochemical assay was reported to detect Helicobacter pylori-specific immunoglobulin G antibodies in 1997 [109]. As anticipated, the LOD of the electrochemical sensor was 7-fold lower than that of the traditional colorimetric methods. A similar result was also found in a later study for rabbit immunoglobulin G detection [99]. To facilitate point-of-care testing, portable and miniaturized screen-printed electrodes were designed to construct electrochemical sensors [104–106,110,111]. In an assay with screen-printed gold electrodes, a LOD of 0.05 μg/L for ochratoxin A detection was achieved, demonstrating a nearly 10-fold increase in sensitivity over the ELISA format (Fig. 9B). Besides, there are also numerous other formats of portable sensing formats, including microelectrodes [101,112] and digital microfluidics [103,113,114], both of which have been successfully employed to construct TMB-based electrochemical sensors.

To facilitate the applications of TMB-based electrochemical sensors in diverse scenarios, aptamers and nanomaterials were also employed. Aptamers, the typical displacement of antibodies, have been successfully applied to detect ochratoxin A and human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 in TMB-based electrochemical sensors [115,116]. Such a substitution can reduce the costs and easily integrate another functional nucleic acid (e.g., G-quadruplex DNAzyme) to construct simple sensors. In comparison to HRP, nanomaterials that possess peroxidase activity have low cost and high stability. Therefore, the label of nanomaterials as peroxidase mimics to construct TMB-based electrochemical sensors were widely reported (Fig. 9C) [114,117–119].

To increase the sensitivity of the electrochemical sensors, the most used strategy is the hybridization chain reaction, which could obtain tens of times sensitivity increase via a target-induced cycle amplification (Fig. 9D) [108,120–122]. Another popular strategy was to increase the ratio of catalyst to analyte through the employment of nanomaterials with high specific surface areas [21,123]. In 2014, Wang et al. found that the oxidized TMB could accumulate at a polymeric liquid membrane electrode through a cation exchanger [124,125]. Such assays were proved to be a new strategy for sensitivity improvement. Overall, the integration signal amplification strategies could further boost sensor sensitivity, thus potentially accelerating the application of TMB-based electrochemical sensors in fields such as medical diagnosis and food safety monitoring.

The TMB-based electrochemical sensors were based on the reduction or oxidation TMB (TMB+• or TMB2+) and could be rapidly read out with high sensitivity through amperometry. A significant advantage of the electrochemical assays over other technologies is the low-cost and simple devices required for signal readout. Recently, the employment of screen-printed electrodes further reduced the size and cost of instruments, making them more suitable for point-of-care testing. However, the labeling of recognition molecules on electrodes is needed, thus the robustness of electrodes is a bottleneck in practical applications.

TMB and its oxidized forms (TMB+• and TMB2+) exhibit unique spectroscopic and electrochemical properties, which have been used to construct non-colorimetric sensing schemes, including fluorescence, IFE, PTE, PAE, SERS, and electrochemical detection. Compared with the colorimetric format, non-colorimetric techniques exhibit two obvious advantages. First, they offer enhanced sensitivity in sensing. Colorimetric sensing, although simple, is limited by its inherent drawback of sensitivity (A = εbC). Methodologically, the sensitivities of fluorescence, SERS, PTE, and electrochemistry are intrinsically higher than that of colorimetry. Besides, the employment of novel materials and signal amplification strategies can further improve the detection sensitivity. Second, they show expanded applications that are beyond the scope of colorimetric sensing. Traditional TMB-based colorimetric sensing is generally confined to in vitro tests, while PTE and PAE of TMB+• could be readily explored for in vivo sensing. Besides, the electrochemical properties of oxidized TMB also facilitate the development of portable devices for POCT. Therefore, the practical applications of TMB-based sensors were largely broadened.

The non-colorimetric applications of TMB, although promising, still deserve further endeavor. First, most non-colorimetric applications still depend on the redox reaction of TMB, which is prone to be affected by the sample matrix in practical applications. Second, the relatively low water solubility of TMB and its optimal oxidation in acidic media (pH ∼ 4.0) restrict its broad use, particularly applications in physiological conditions. Third, although the spectroscopic properties of TMB and its oxidized forms are superior to those of the benzidine derivatives [16], there is still room for improvement, such as increasing the molar extinction coefficient and extending the absorption bands to NIR-II, which was particularly useful for applications related to IFE, PTE, and PAE. Finally, precipitation of CTCs may occur at high pH, whereas at low pH, it would be converted into TMB2+ via a disproportionation reaction. Thus, the pH-dependent stability of CTCs could also affect its performance in non-colorimetric applications.

To address the above challenges, the following two strategies may be feasible. First, exploring and utilizing the novel characteristics of TMB and its oxidized forms may lead to significant advancements in sensor development. For instance, the in situ self-assembly of TMB+• and TMB2+ revealed unique spectroscopic and electrochemical properties that may be employed in designing innovative sensors [126,127]. Another notable example is the use of TMB2+ to etch AuNRs, which resulted in the change of AuNRs colors [128,129]. Second, new TMB derivatives with enhanced sensing performances are expected. For example, a recent example showed that substituting the two -CH3 groups of TMB with -OCH3 resulted in substantial red-shifting of the NIR-II absorption and enhanced absorptivity at 1064 nm, thus allowing the use of 1064 nm laser for improved photothermal immunoassay [38]. Third, the employment of nanomaterials to encapsulate TMB, such as liposomes and mesoporous silica, may be a promising approach to mitigate the effects of pH and solubility, therefore extending non-colorimetric sensing. Considering the similar origin of PTE and PAE, the above derivative may be also promising in PAE-based applications. Overall, TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing have greatly broadened the sensing toolbox. Further exploration of TMB's properties and its derivatives could accelerate the development and popularization of TMB-based non-colorimetric sensing applications.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ma, Y. Lin, L. Guo, B. Qiu, G. Chen, H.-h Yang, et al., A universal multicolor immunosensor for semiquantitative visual detection of biomarkers with the naked eyes, Biosens. Bioelectron., 87(2017) 122-128.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xianming Li is currently a Postdoc at West China Hospital, Sichuan University. His research focuses on optical probes for clinical diagnosis.
Ling Li is currently an undergraduate student of Sichuan University. Her research focuses on TMB derivative-based optical probes.
Honghu Tang is research associate at West China Hospital, Sichuan University. Her research focuses on clinical diagnosis of autoimmune diseases.
Chengxie Xie is currently a Postdoc at West China Hospital, Sichuan University. Her research focuses on clinical diagnosis.
Yi Zhao is currently a professor at West China Hospital, Sichuan University. His research focuses on innate immunity and the mechanisms of autoimmune diseases.

Peng Wu is a professor at Analytical & Testing Center and College of Chemistry, Sichuan University. His research interests are mainly spectroscopy and nanomaterials for analytical chemistry, with over 70 publications on the leading journals of analytical chemistry and related.
